blocks.content: The room was the same room it always was, but something had shifted inside the being of it. Not the furniture, not the dark — the quality of being alone in a space after something has moved through you and not fully passed.
Cassandra lay on her back and looked at the ceiling and did not try to sleep.

Maya's last messages were still on the phone. She had set it face-down but she could feel them through the plastic, the way you feel weather through glass — the fact of it even when you're not looking. She knew. She had always known. Maya had been loving her like that since they were children, long before either of them had a reason to, long before loving her meant something you had to sustain across the knowledge of what was coming.

She wasn't angry. That was the thing she wanted to be clear about, even just to herself in the dark. She wasn't frightened tonight in the same way Maya was frightened, and she wasn't going to lie there cataloguing everything that was wrong. She had made a decision about that years ago. It was the most important decision she'd ever made, and she had to keep making it.
But tonight it was harder to make.

She turned onto her side and looked at the window — the familiar slant of streetlight across the wall, the shapes she'd memorized in childhood, the way this particular room held its dark differently than anywhere else she'd ever slept. She had lived her whole life in proximity to this specific darkness.

It had been an ordinary Tuesday morning in June. She remembered it specifically — Tuesday, because she'd had an appointment that morning and had needed to be outside afterward. Needed the particular medicine of open air and no specific direction. The park was the place she always went when she needed to exist without agenda.

She'd had iced coffee in a paper cup and was sitting on the low stone wall near the fountain — not really doing anything, just being somewhere, which she had learned was its own legitimate purpose — when she noticed the boy with the sketchbook.

He was probably fifteen feet away. Close enough to observe but not close enough to interrupt. He was sitting at a slight angle to the fountain, not facing it directly, which she would later understand was typical of him — he rarely looked at things head-on. He preferred the angle that let him see the thing it was pretending not to be.
He was drawing with the total absorption of someone who had genuinely forgotten about being seen.
She watched him for a few minutes. Then she slid off the wall and walked over.
